coredata.dc:title: Construction, energy transfer, tunable multicolor and luminescence enhancement of YF3:RE3+(RE=Eu, Tb)/carbon dots nanocomposites
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yali
Hong, Feng
Yu, Liuyang
Xu, Haiping
Liu, Guixia
Dong, Xiangting
Yu, Wensheng
Wang, Jinxian
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have displayed giant potential in carbon-based multifunctional nanomaterials owing to their outstanding optical properties. The nanocomposites combining CDs with rare-earth (RE) luminescence materials have increasingly attracted more and more attention due to the dual-mode optical properties. In this work, YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites were synthesized via hydrothermal method. The phase structure, surface functional groups, morphology and elements distribution of the samples were measured by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), respectively. Besides, optical properties have also been systematically studied by photoluminescence spectra. The energy transfer of YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites from CDs to Eu3+ was proved. The YF3:Ln3+/CDs nanocomposites realized luminescence enhancement and tunable multicolor. Furthermore, using CDs as the blue light source, YF3:Eu3+ as the red light source and YF3:Tb3+ as the green light source, the YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites can achieve white light emission with CIE coordinate (0.3117, 0.2791) upon ultraviolet-light (318 nm) excitation, suggesting that this nanocomposites can be highly applied to the bioimaging and solid-state laser.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Tunable multicolor
Luminescence
Rare earth
Energy transfer
Nanocomposites

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Tunable color luminescence and CIE chromaticity coordinates

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of CDs (a), YF3:Eu3+ (b), YF3:Eu3+/CDs (c), YF3:Eu3+/Tb3+/CDs (d) and the standard card of YF3 (PDF#74–0911) is shown as reference.
SEM images and EDS spectra of YF3:Eu3+ (a, b) and YF3:Eu3+/CDs (c, d) samples.
FT-IR spectra of CDs (a), YF3:Eu3+/CDs (b) and YF3:Eu3+ (c).

Photoluminescence excitation (λem = 439 nm, a) and emission spectra (λex = 350 nm, b) of CDs. Emission spectra of 10 mg/mL CDs solution (c), 70 mg/mL CDs solution (e) and 500 mg/mL CDs solution (g). Corresponding to the relationship between the emission intensity of above concentration CDs at 439 nm and different excitation wavelengths (d, f, and h).
PL spectra of YF3:x%Eu3+ nanoparticles excited at a wavelength of 395 nm. The insert is the corresponding integrated emission intensity of YF3:x%Eu3+.
PLE spectra of YF3:Eu3+ and YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanoparticles monitored at 595 nm.
PL spectra of YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+ nanoparticles excited at a wavelength of 318 nm.
PLE spectrum of YF3:Eu3+ nanoparticles monitored at 595 nm and PL spectrum of CDs solution excited at 318 nm.
Emission mechanism diagram of the YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites.

(a) PL spectra of YF3:yEu3+/(150 μL) CDs nanocomposites excited at 318 nm, (b) the emission intensity of CDs (425 nm) and Eu3+ (595 nm) as a function of Eu3+ concentrations. (c) CIE chromaticity diagram of the YF3:yEu3+/(150 μL) CDs nanocomposites, 1% Eu3+ (point 1), 2% Eu3+ (point 2), 4% Eu3+ (point 3), 6% Eu3+ (point 4), respectively.

(a) PL spectra of YF3:8%Eu3+/xCDs (x=10 μL, 50 μL, 100 μL and 150 μL) nanocomposites excited at 318 nm, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of the YF3:8%Eu3+@(x μL)CDs nanocomposites, (10 μL) CDs (point 6), (50 μL) CDs (point 7), (100 μL) CDs (point 8) and (150 μL) CDs (point 9), pure CDs (point 5), and YF3:8%Eu3+ (point 10), respectively.

(a) PL spectra of YF3:8%Eu3+,x%Tb3+/CD (150 μL) (x=2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12) nanocomposites excited at 318 nm, (b) CIE chromaticity diagram of the YF3:8%Eu3+,x%Tb3+@(150 μL)CDs nanocomposites, 2% Tb3+ (point 14), 4% Tb3+ (point 15), 6% Tb3+ (point 16), 8% Tb3+ (point 17), 10% Tb3+ (point 18), 12% Tb3+ (point 19), pure CDs (point 11), YF3:Tb3+(point 12) and YF3:Eu3+(point 13), respectively.
CIE chromaticity coordinate for YF3:yEu3+/CDs (150 μL) nanocomposites under excitation at 318 nm.
CIE chromaticity coordinate for pure CDs, YF3:8%Eu3+ and YF3:8%Eu3+/(x μL)CDs nanopcomposites under excitation wavelengths at 318 nm.
CIE chromaticity coordinate for pure CDs, YF3:Tb3+, YF3:Eu3+ and YF3:8%Eu3+, x%Tb3+/CDs (150 μL) nanocomposites under excitation at 318 nm.

head.ce:title: Construction, energy transfer, tunable multicolor and luminescence enhancement of YF3:RE3+(RE=Eu, Tb)/carbon dots nanocomposites

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Applied Chemistry and Nanotechnology at University of Jilin Province, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Applied Chemistry and Nanotechnology at University of Jilin Province
Changchun University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Applied Chemistry and Nanotechnology at University of Jilin Province, Changchun University of Science and Technology, Changchun, 130022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have displayed giant potential in carbon-based multifunctional nanomaterials owing to their outstanding optical properties. The nanocomposites combining CDs with rare-earth (RE) luminescence materials have increasingly attracted more and more attention due to the dual-mode optical properties. In this work, YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites were synthesized via hydrothermal method. The phase structure, surface functional groups, morphology and elements distribution of the samples were measured by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) and Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS), respectively. Besides, optical properties have also been systematically studied by photoluminescence spectra. The energy transfer of YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites from CDs to Eu3+ was proved. The YF3:Ln3+/CDs nanocomposites realized luminescence enhancement and tunable multicolor. Furthermore, using CDs as the blue light source, YF3:Eu3+ as the red light source and YF3:Tb3+ as the green light source, the YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites can achieve white light emission with CIE coordinate (0.3117, 0.2791) upon ultraviolet-light (318 nm) excitation, suggesting that this nanocomposites can be highly applied to the bioimaging and solid-state laser.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, people pay more and more attention to environmental protection and sustainable development of resources. As a kind of material with low toxicity, photo-stability and excellent water solubility, carbon dots (CDs) have been attracted increasingly attention. Besides, the broad excitation and emission peaks make CDs be used in diverse fields, such as bio-imaging, bio-medicine, solar cells, printing inks, catalysis, light-emitting devices [1–4] and anti-counterfeiting [5–10]. Up to now, CDs have been prepared by various physical and chemical methods, including laser ablation, hydrothermal carbonization, microwave assisted and pyrolysis [11–14]. The hydrothermal method is a cost-effective and environmentally friendly way for synthesizing CDs. For example, Jiang and co-workers [15] prepared red, green and blue multicolor emitting carbon dots via a facile hydrothermal method, and pointed out that the multicolor emission was due to the difference of surface energy states and size for CDs. However, CDs powders are hardly obtained because of their hygroscopicity features. Furthermore, the excessive Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and π-π interaction can directly induce the self-quenching, which handicap their applications and developments in many aspects such as light emitting materials and multicolor display. In order to settle this problem, the construction of nanocomposites is an excellent strategy [16–22]. Recently, some researches have been reported on the composites combining carbon dots and rare earth luminescence materials. For example, Liu [23] and coworkers proposed that by trimming the ratio of Eu3+ complex to CDs, the as-obtained composites can realize the light modulation. It lays a solid foundation for the application of carbon dots in the fields of biotechnology, OLED devices, laser and optical amplification. Xu group [24] reported the NaYF4:Eu@CDs composites with dual emission characteristics. It can be used to construct a colorimetric fluorescence probe. Compared with the traditional fluorescence quenching method, it has higher precision. Moreover, by adjusting the ratio of CDs to Eu3+, the composite shows great potential in multicolor display and white LED. Later, their group also investigated the properties and applications of NaYF4:Yb,Er(Tm)@CDs composites, which has opened up a new view for the development of up-conversion luminescent materials, especially for the application of biological field [25]. Although the previous efforts have achieved some results, however, the exploration and application of these composite materials are still in the primary stage, which needs further research and improvement.

As a familiar luminescent material, lanthanide doped fluorides have been widely used in the optics field due to brilliant stability, narrow emission and long fluorescence lifetime [26,27]. Among the fluoride material, yttrium trifluoride (YF3) is an excellent host for the up/down conversion luminescent materials [28,29]. Y3+ can be effortlessly superseded by other trivalent RE3+ ions without additional charge compensation. At present, enhancing the luminescence intensity and controlling the multicolor of rare-earth ions doped YF3 have become hot topic. As we know, adjusting the morphology and size of the samples can improve the luminescent properties [30–32]. In addition, the luminescence intensity can be enhanced and the multicolor emission can be realized based on the energy transfer [33–37]. So, the combination of CDs and Ln3+ doped YF3 luminescent materials is expected to enhance luminescence and multicolor emission through energy transfer. In addition, through the preparation of YF3:Ln3+/CDs nanomaterials, the solid-state quenching of CDS can be solved, which provides the possibility for the application of CDs in the field of optical devices.

In this work, YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites were obtained through hydrothermal synthesis method. The photoluminescence spectra of CDs and YF3:Ln3+ under UV light excitation have been obtained. The luminescence mechanism between CDs and Eu3+ in nanocomposites under 318 nm light excitation have been discussed. The nanocomposites can realize multicolor tunable luminescence by varying the contents of Ln3+ (Tb3+, Eu3+) and CDs.

Y2O3 (purity>99.99%), Eu2O3 (purity>99.99%), Tb4O7 (purity>99.99%), NH4F (purity>96.0%) and ethanol (C2H5OH, purity≥95%) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., citric acid (C6H8O7·H2O, purity>99.8%), urea (H2NCONH2, purity>99.0%) were purchased from China Bei Jing Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd., deionized water (DI water) was taken as the common solvent in total experiment. All chemicals were directly used in experiments without further purification.

CDs were fabricated using previous method with some revisions [38]. First, urea and citric acid with molar ratio of 2:1 utilized as nitrogen source and carbon source were put in 20 mL deionized water with violent stirring until they were completely dissolved. Subsequently, the solution was slowly transferred into a 50 mL Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted for 4 h at 160 °C. The solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm for 25 min to further purify the sample. Finally, the supernatant solution of CDs was maintained at 4 °C for the next step.

The corresponding rare earth oxides were dissolved in HNO3 solution and stirred continuously. The remaining HNO3 was dislodged by heating and evaporating to obtain Eu(NO3)3 (0.05 mol L−1), Tb(NO3)3 (0.05 mol L−1) and Y(NO3)3 (0.1 mol L−1) aqueous solutions, respectively.

YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites were produced by hydrothermal route. The preparing procedure is as followed. First, Y(NO3)3 (9.2 mL) and Eu(NO3)3 (1.6 mL) were added into a beaker to form a mixture solution. Afterward, the CDs aqueous solution was put in the mixture solution with continuous agitation for 15 min and 3 mmol NH4F solid was poured into the solution. Then, the emerald solution was stirred rapidly. Subsequently, the solution was slowly transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless autoclave and reacted for 24 h at 180 °C. The precipitate was washed and separated with deionized water and ethanol by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min. Then the sample was obtained by drying in air at 60 °C overnight in oven. The synthesis procedure of the YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs was similar to that of the YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites, except for using different amount of RE3+.

The phase structure of all samples was recorded on X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), ranged from 10° to 90° under a scanning speed of 10° min−1 with Cu Κα radiation (λ=1.5406 Å). The photoluminescence excitation (PLE) and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were measured by fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI F-7000) with the excitation and emission slits of 2.5 nm in the nature environment. To acquire morphology and elements composition of samples. Field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM) equipped with an Energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS) was used. The surface functional groups determinations data were performed on a fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Japan) with the range of 4000–500 cm−1.

The crystal phases of CDs, YF3:Eu3+, YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs are measured by X-ray powder diffraction. As described in Fig. 1 a, the XRD pattern of CDs displays a broad peak centered at 2θ = 25.6°, indicating that CDs are amorphous structure [1,39]. From Fig. 1b-d, all peaks of the as-obtained samples are corresponded to the pure YF3 phase (PDF #74–0911) with an orthorhombic symmetry of Pnma and lattice parameters of a = 6.353 Å, b = 6.850 Å and c = 4.393 Å. We cannot find impurity or other phase, indicating that the introducing of doped Eu3+, Tb3+ ions and carbon dots does not change the YF3 phase purity. However, in Fig. 1d, the diffraction peak of carbon dots was not found. This may be attributed to two factors: (1) the carbon dots are amorphous and the amount of carbon dots added is small; (2) the strong crystallinity of YF3 covers the diffraction peak of carbon dots.

The morphology and size of YF3:Eu3+ and YF3:Eu3+/CDs are recorded by FE-SEM. As detected in Fig. 2 a, the pure YF3:Eu3+ nanocrystal exhibits an octahedron-like morphology with smooth surface. The length and diameter of the sample are about 258 nm and 434 nm, respectively. Fig. 2b displays the corresponding EDS spectrum, the F, Eu and Y elements can be obviously recognized. As shown in Fig. 2c, the morphology of YF3:Eu3+/CDs composites exhibits a sphere shape with the diameter of 354 nm and rough surfaces. Fig. 2d displays the corresponding EDS spectrum, the C, N, O, F, Eu and Y elements can be easily recognized. The C, N and O elements are stemmed from carbon dots, which shows that the CDs are successfully coupled with YF3:Eu3+ nanoparticles. The peak of Si element resulted from the silicon wafer in the measurement.

In order to explore the surface functional group of samples, FT-IR spectra of CDs, YF3:Eu3+ and YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites are measured. As displayed in Fig. 3 , the absorption peaks of 3000–3700 cm−1 are corresponded to the stretching vibration of –OH group in H2O in all samples, which is mainly due to the surface absorbed water of the samples. The FT-IR spectrum of YF3:Eu3+/CDs shows the absorption peaks at about 2360 cm−1, 2340 cm−1, 1577 cm−1 and 1406 cm−1, corresponding to the –CN, –CC, –CO and N–H/CC groups of CDs, respectively, indicating that carbon dots are formed through the carbonization process of urea and citric acid. Because the carbon dots are composed of carboxyl and amino functional groups [40–42], it has strong complexing ability.

In order to examine the luminescence properties of CDs, the photoluminescence spectra are measured at room temperature. Fig. 4 a displays the excitation spectrum by monitoring at 439 nm. Two peaks at 249 and 350 nm are ascribed to the π-π* transition and the n-π* transition of functional groups in CDs, respectively [43–45]. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the maximum emission position is located at 439 nm under 350 nm light excitation. We have also measured the emission spectra of CDs with different concentrations, as depicted in Fig. 4(c-e-g). Moreover, Fig. 4(d-f-h) display the relationship of the emission intensity at 439 nm and different excitation wavelengths. With the excitation wavelength increasing from 300 to 390 nm, the strongest emission position at 439 nm of all samples does not change, which shows that the as-obtained CDs still exhibit excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence performances. However, the fluorescence intensity can be changed by adjusting the concentrations of carbon dots due to the inner filter effect (IFE) [46].

The influences of Eu3+ content in YF3 host on the photoluminescence performance are investigated. Fig. 5 describes the emission spectra of the obtained YF3:x%Eu3+ (x=1, 2, 4, 6, 8 and 10) under 395 nm excitation. As Eu3+ doping concentration increases from 1% to 8%, the fluorescence intensity increases. Then, the emission intensity achieves the maximum at 8% doping concentration. When the content of Eu3+ ions is beyond 8%, the luminescence intensity gradually decreases due to the concentration quenching effect.

The excitation spectra of YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+ phosphors are displayed in Fig. 6 . By monitoring at 595 nm, the PLE spectrum of YF3:Eu3+ is composed of many sharp peaks such as 318 and 395 nm, which are corresponded to the Eu3+ ions typical intra-configurational f-f transitions of 7F0–5H3 (318 nm) and 7F0-5L6 (395 nm). Compared with YF3:Eu3+, the excitation spectrum of YF3:Eu3+/CDs, except for the corresponding absorption peak of Eu3+, has an obvious broad and strong band of 200–400 nm, which is ascribed to the transitions from π-π* and n-π* of CDs [47–49]. The results powerfully prove the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ in YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites. Furthermore, the YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites show stronger excitation peak compared with the bare YF3: Eu3+ nanoparticle. Besides, the near-UV wavelength of 318 and 395 nm can be used to simultaneously excite CDs and YF3: Eu3+ nanoparticle.

Fig. 7 displays the photoluminescence spectra of the YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+ nanomaterials under 318 nm excitation. The broad emission band of 350–550 nm is corresponded to the emission of CDs. Compared with Figs. 4 and 7 shows that when CDs combine with YF3:Eu3+, the strongest emission peak of CDs shifts to 425 from 439 nm under 318 nm excitation. It may be owing to the change of CDs from solution to composite powder [25]. Moreover, the YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+ samples show three sharp emission bands in the red region, which are assigned to 5D0-7F0 (584 nm), 5D0-7F1 (595 nm) and 5D0-7F2 (616 nm) transitions of Eu3+. Furthermore, the luminescence intensity of the YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites is significantly enhanced, which is derived from energy transfer between CDs and Eu3+ ions. To further validate the energy transfer, we measured the PL spectrum of CDs and the PLE spectrum of YF3:Eu3+. As depicted in Fig. 8 , it shows that the absorption peaks of Eu3+ ions at 410, 460 and 527 nm overlap with the strong emission band of 400–530 nm of CDs, which further proves the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+ ions.

The luminescence and energy transfer processes between CDs and YF3:Eu3+ are neatly shown in Fig. 9 . Firstly, the CDs could absorb the UV light, and the electrons are transferred from C π to C π* energy level. Then, the electrons are relaxed to lowest LUMO energy level with C π*-N π* and N π*-O π* process. When the electrons transit to the C π energy level by blue emission and energy transfer to Eu3+ ions. Meanwhile, the Eu3+ ions absorb the photon energy and produce the transition from 7F0 to 5D4 energy level. Through the nonradiative transitions process, the electrons relax to the 5D0 energy level, and finally transfer to 7F0 (584 nm), 7F1 (595 nm) and 7F2 (616 nm) energy level, respectively. In addition, the electrons of excited energy at 5D1-4 levels of Eu3+ were captured by CDs and then diverted to 5D0 energy level by FRET process for YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites. Thus, the number of electrons in 5D0 level of Eu3+ increases. It is concluded that the energy loss of Eu3+ nonradiative transition (from 5D4 to 5D0) is greatly reduced by using the ability of CDs electron capture (EC) [1]. At the same time, the energy transfer (ET) between CDs and Eu3+ improve the emission intensities of 584, 595 and 616 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Tunable color luminescence and CIE chromaticity coordinates
ce:section.ce:para: In order to fully explore the emission color of the samples, we prepared a series of YF3: y%Eu3+/150 μLCDs (y = 1, 2, 4, 6) nanocomposites. The photoluminescence spectra are shown in Fig. 10 a, all samples display the emission peaks of the CDs and Eu3+ ions under 318 nm excitation. However, with the change of Eu3+ content, the relative intensities of emission peaks are significantly different. From Fig. 10b, the CDs content is constant, but the emission intensity of 425 nm (CDs) obviously decreases with the increase of Eu3+ content (1–6%). While the luminescence intensity at 595 nm (Eu3+) gradually increases. These results confirm that the emission color of samples will change by adjusting the Eu3+ content. The Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of YF3: y%Eu3+/150 μLCDs are displayed in Fig. 10c and Table 1 . For the CIE diagram of YF3: y%Eu3+/150 μLCDs nanocomposites, the chromaticity coordinates (x, y) can change from point 1 (0.1720, 0.1637) to point 4 (0.2134, 0.2007) with the increase of Eu3+ content. Furthermore, the color hue of these powers is changed gradually from dark blue to purplish blue. The digital photographs are depicted in the illustration of Fig. 10c.

To further investigate the effect of CDs contents on the color hue for the samples, we prepared a series of YF3:8%Eu3+/(x μL) CDs (x=10, 50, 100 and 150). Fig. 11 a displays the PL spectra of YF3:8%Eu3+/xCDs under 318 nm excitation. By increasing the CDs content, the emission intensity of CDs arises first decreases later. Whereas, the corresponding emission intensity of Eu3+ ions gradually increases. These results show that the luminescence intensity of samples will change by adjusting the concentrations of CDs. The Commission International de L'Eclairage (CIE) chromaticity coordinates of YF3:8%Eu3+/(x μL)CDs are displayed in Fig. 11b and Table 2 . The CIE chromaticity coordinate of pure Eu3+ activated YF3 is point 10 (0.5382, 0.4487), and that of the pure CDs is located at the point 5 (0.1499, 0.0785), corresponding to orange yellow and dark blue, respectively. For the CIE diagram of YF3:8%Eu3+/(x μL)CDs nanocomposites, the chromaticity coordinates (x, y) can vary from point 6 (0.1696, 0.1185) to point 9 (0.2806, 0.2327) with increasing the amount of CDs (from 10 to 150 μL). Furthermore, the color hue of these powers is changed gradually from purplish blue to purple. The digital photographs are shown in the inset of Fig. 11b.

Tb3+ ion is an important activator, which can be doped in many host lattices, leading to strong green emission. In order to achieve the near white light emission, we prepared a series of YF3:8%Eu3+,x%Tb3+/CDs (x=2, 4, 6, 8,10 and 12) samples. Fig. 12 a displays the PL spectra of YF3:8%Eu3+,x%Tb3+/CDs (150 μL) under the excitation of 318 nm. The emission peaks of the CDs (350–500 nm) and Eu3+ ions (595 and 616 nm) are observed. New emission peaks are also obtained at 488 nm and 542 nm, which are corresponded to 5D4-7F6 and 5D4-7F5 transitions of Tb3+. However, the relative emission intensity has a significant change with the increase of Tb3+ content (2–12%). Hence, the emission color of samples will change. The CIE chromaticity coordinates of YF3:Eu3+,x%Tb3+/CDs (150 μL) nanocomposites are determined according to their PL spectra, which are displayed in the CIE diagram of Fig. 12b. The data are shown in Table 3 . The pure CDs, YF3:Tb3+ and YF3:Eu3+ coordinates are (0.1499, 0.0785), (0.2732, 0.5311) and (0.5382, 0.4487), which correspond to the dark blue (Point 11), yellow green (Point 12) and orange yellow (Point 13), respectively. When Tb3+ is added into the nanocomposites, the green component could be increased. So, the nanocomposites can be achieved the multicolor emission (from point 14 to point 19) by adjusting the Tb3+ contents. The chromaticity coordinates (x, y) can vary from point 14 (0.2600, 0.2389) to point 19 (0.2219, 0.2703), which correspond to the color hue transformation from brilliant blue through white light to greenish blue. In particular, the nanocomposite of YF3:8%Eu3+,10%Tb3+/CDs (150 μL) exhibits nearly white emission (0.3117, 0.2791) under 318 nm excitation. The digital photographs are also shown in the illustration of Fig. 12b. The results further demonstrate that the YF3:Ln3+/CDs nanocomposite can be used in the fields of white LED devices based on ultraviolet excitation.

To sum up, we have successfully fabricated YF3:Eu3+/CDs and YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites by a facile hydrothermal route. The YF3:Eu3+/CDs nanocomposites can emit both blue (CDs) and red light (YF3: Eu3+) simultaneously. Furthermore, the emission intensity of Eu3+ is enhanced due to the energy transfer from CDs to Eu3+. In addition, the multicolor luminescence of YF3:Eu3+/CDs can be realized by tuning the relative amounts of CDs and Eu3+. Finally, the prepared YF3:Eu3+,Tb3+/CDs nanocomposites achieve the near white light emission by adjusting the amount of CDs and Ln3+ under 318 nm excitation. The nanocomposites are confirmed to be a very potential material for White LED, anti-counterfeiting and solid-state devices.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of P. R. China (NSFC) (Grant No. 51072026, 51573023) and the Development of science and technology plan projects of Jilin province (Grant No. 20170101185JC).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strategy to enhance the luminescence of lanthanide ions doped MgWO4 nanosheets through incorporation of carbon dots
Inorg. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.B. Huang, W. Lu, J. Wang, Q.F. Li, B.S. Tian, C.Y. Li, Z.L. Wang, J. Jin, J.H. Hao, Strategy to enhance the luminescence of lanthanide ions doped MgWO4 nanosheets through incorporation of carbon dots. Inorg. Chem. 57 (2018) 8662-8672. https://doi:10.1021/acs.inorgchem.8b01592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and their applications
J. Mater. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.V. Medeiros, J. Manioudakis, F. Noun, J.R. Macairan, F. Victoria, R. Naccache, Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots and their applications. J. Mater. Chem. C 7 (2019) 7175-7195. https://doi.org/10.1039/C9TC01640F.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on syntheses, properties, characterization and bioanalytical applications of fluorescent carbon dots
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P.L. Zuo, X.H. Lu, Z.G. Sun, Y.H. Guo, H. He, A review on syntheses, properties, characterization and bioanalytical applications of fluorescent carbon dots. Microchim. Acta 183 (2016) 519-542. https://doi:10.1007/s00604-015-1705-3.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A sensitive and low toxicity electrochemical sensor for 2,4-dichlorophenol based on the nanocomposite of carbon dots, hexadecyltrimethyl ammonium bromide and chitosan
Sens. Actuators, B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.L. Yu, X. Yue, R. Yang, S.S. Jing, L.B.
